Moralis • UX Case Study

Redesigning Web3 Trading: Making It Finally Make Sense

A UX Case Study On Account Abstraction And Trade Widget

Role

Lead Product Designer

Timeline

2022 – 2025

Team

Cross-functional

Skills

Product Design

UX Research

Web3 UX

Overview

Moralis Money helps crypto users discover tokens before they trend — but once they did, trading them felt slow, complex, and disconnected. With a growing user base of active token hunters, the gap between discovery and trade was costing us conversions every day.

The business context

Moralis Money had built a strong discovery engine — users could spot trending tokens early. But the product stopped short of the moment that mattered most: the trade itself. Every user who left to complete a trade on a DEX was a potential churned user.

My role

Lead a design exploration that solved two challenges: simplify the end-to-end trading experience, and make account abstraction — smart contract-based trading accounts — understandable and usable for mainstream crypto users.

What is account abstraction? Smart contract wallets that eliminate seed phrases and MetaMask pop-ups — enabling a smoother, more controlled trading experience that traditional EOA wallets can't offer.

From Discovery to Drop-off

Why Were Users Not Completing Trades — And What Was It Costing Us.

The experience (Before)

Users came to Moralis to discover trending tokens...But when it came time to trading, they had to:

Copy the token address

Manually copy the contract address from Moralis before they could do anything else.

Leave Moralis and open a DEX

Navigate away to a separate platform like Uniswap to actually place the trade.

Reconnect their wallet

Re-authenticate on the new platform, adding yet another friction point.

Search for the token again

Re-find the same token they had already discovered on Moralis.

Manually complete the trade

Finally execute the trade after navigating a completely separate product flow.

Before vs. After

Five steps across two platforms became a single, in-context action.

But it was slow, clunky, and confusing.

Result? Users Dropped Off Before Ever Making A Trade. Every Exit Meant Lost Conversions And Reduced Retention.

And Reimagined The Entire Trading Journey From Scratch.

Discovery & Insights

Research that drove our design direction.

We ran user interviews and flow analytics to understand pain points. We learned:

Users didn't understand why they needed to activate a trading account

The concept of account abstraction was invisible — users had no mental model for what a "trading account" was or why it was different from their existing wallet. Without that context, the activation step felt like a bug, not a feature.

Multiple steps created friction

Copying addresses, switching platforms, MetaMask pop-ups — each step was a potential exit point that eroded user confidence and completion rates. The more steps between discovery and trade, the lower the conversion.

These insights drove our design direction.

Solution

Designing a Seamless Web3 Trading Experience

From Confusion To Clarity — How We Made Trading Intuitive Again In Three Steps.

#1 — Widget

We created an embedded trade widget users could access from any screen. We A/B tested it against a fullscreen version and found the sidebar format increased trading activity and kept users in context.

#2 — Sidebar Experience

We presented a trading sidebar accessible from any Moralis Money screen. The sidebar includes fields for selecting the buy and sell tokens, integrating our token search (so users were able to find trending tokens and immediately trade them).

#3 — Account Abstraction Onboarding

Onboarding flow designed to deploy and activate the "Trading Account" via account abstraction. We guide the user from the welcome screen to depositing funds and confirming that their smart account is ready, reducing friction with clear messages and animations.

The key design decision: explain what a trading account does for you, not what it is technically. Users don't need to understand smart contracts — they need to understand why this unlocks a better trading experience.

Achievements

During this exploration, we conducted usability tests with prototypes across multiple rounds of testing. The results were clear: participants who went through the guided onboarding understood the trading account concept significantly better than those who encountered the previous experience — which simply launched a technical MetaMask window with no explanation or context.

Higher comprehension

Most participants understood what a trading account was and why they needed it — a sharp improvement over the baseline experience where users were left confused by a raw MetaMask prompt.

Fewer drop-offs during activation

The guided onboarding flow reduced abandonment at the account activation step, the highest-friction point in the original flow.

A/B test validation

The sidebar widget format outperformed the fullscreen version in trading activity — users stayed in context and completed more trades.

More engagement. Higher conversions. A Foundation For Advanced Trading.

Our design exploration significantly improved the user experience and product strategy of Moralis. We collaborated with the product team to envision a more advanced trading experience within Moralis, leveraging the flexibility of the smart trading account.

What's Next

With a validated foundation in place, we worked with the product team to explore what a more advanced trading experience could look like — one that served power users without sacrificing the simplicity we had built for newcomers.

What if we provided users with professional-grade tools while maintaining simplicity?

High-impact business ideas emerged from sessions with the product team and power-user interviews: enabling automatic orders based on price alerts, copy-trading (mimicking expert wallets' trades), and customizable panels with market data.

What if we provided users a standalone trading screen, with customizable widgets?

We explored a dedicated trading screen where users could configure their own workspace: live trading pair chart, user's "saved tokens" list, trending tokens, recent relevant transactions, among others. Early concept testing with active traders showed strong interest in this direction.

Learnings

This initiative not only expanded the functionality of Moralis Money but also solidified its position as an essential tool for traders and developers in the crypto ecosystem. Here are some key learnings from my experience:

In Web3, the mental model gap is the real UX problem.

Users don't need to understand smart contracts — they need to understand what they unlock. Every time we explained account abstraction in technical terms, we lost users. Every time we framed it around the benefit ("no more MetaMask pop-ups, no more copying addresses"), they leaned in. The design challenge wasn't the UI — it was the translation.

Context collapse kills conversions.

Asking users to leave your product to complete its core action is a bet you will almost always lose. Bringing trading in-context — into the sidebar, into the same screen where they discovered the token — wasn't just a UX improvement. It was a product strategy shift that kept users inside the Moralis ecosystem at the most valuable moment.

Onboarding is product positioning.

The account abstraction onboarding wasn't just a setup flow — it was the moment users decided whether they trusted Moralis with their trades. How we explained the trading account shaped how users thought about the entire product. Getting that framing right was as important as getting the UI right.

The trading widget and account abstraction onboarding were handed off to engineering and integrated into the Moralis Money product roadmap.

Designed + Coded with

by Tatiana
